1:
"These are the words of the covenant, which the LORD commanded Moses to make with the children of Israel in the land of Moab, beside the covenant which he made with them in Horeb." The witnesses note a fresh covenant made in Moab, beside the one given at Horeb. The witnesses note that Gill notes "these are the words of the covenant" looks not to what goes before but to what follows in the next chapters, full of promises of grace to Jew and Gentile. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set these as the addresses renewing the covenant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the words of the covenant — reaching to the promises of grace; the made in Moab — a fresh renewal; the beside Horeb — joined to the first covenant; the renewed in each generation — the bond entered anew (Jer. 31:31).

The witnesses agree the verse opens a solemn renewal of the covenant on the plains of Moab, distinct from yet joined to that of Horeb: a new generation, about to enter the land, was bound afresh to God; Gill marks that "the words of the covenant" reach forward into the promises of grace that follow, to Jew and Gentile alike; the renewal shows that God's covenant is to be taken up by each generation as its own, the bond with him entered anew at every great turning; the same God who covenanted at Sinai covenants again in Moab, his faithfulness binding his people to himself afresh (cf. Jer. 31:31).

So the witnesses make the verse the covenant in Moab: The witnesses note the the words of the covenant: reaching to the promises of grace. The witnesses note the made in Moab: a fresh renewal. The witnesses note the beside Horeb: joined to the first covenant. The witnesses note the renewed in each generation: the bond entered anew (Jer. 31:31). The expositors observe the the words of the covenant: reaching to the promises of grace. The expositors observe the made in Moab: a fresh renewal. The expositors observe the beside Horeb: joined to the first covenant. The expositors observe the renewed in each generation: the bond entered anew (Jer. 31:31). The believer is taught to mark the the words of the covenant, the made in Moab, the beside Horeb, the renewed in each generation, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that a fresh covenant was made in Moab beside that of Horeb — God's covenant taken up by the new generation, the bond with him renewed afresh, pointing on to the promises of grace (Jer. 31:31).
2:
"And Moses called unto all Israel, and said unto them, Ye have seen all that the LORD did before your eyes in the land of Egypt unto Pharaoh, and unto all his servants, and unto all his land;" The witnesses note Moses summoning all Israel and rehearsing the mighty works they had seen in Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes he summoned the whole body of the people, a solemn covenant being to be made. The witnesses note that they had seen all the LORD did in Egypt before their eyes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the called all Israel — the whole people summoned; the ye have seen — their own remembrance; the what the LORD did in Egypt — his judgments on Pharaoh; the memory the spur — the seen mercy binding to obey (Rom. 12:1).

The witnesses agree Moses grounded the covenant in the people's own remembrance of God's mighty acts: he called "all Israel" and set before them what their own eyes had seen — the LORD's judgments on Pharaoh and all Egypt; the appeal to memory makes the covenant a reasonable response to known mercy, not a blind submission; it teaches that the obligation to serve God rests on what he has done and we have witnessed, the remembrance of his works the spur to obedience, "I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God" (Rom. 12:1), the seen deliverance binding the heart.

So the witnesses make the verse ye have seen: The witnesses note the called all Israel: the whole people summoned. The witnesses note the ye have seen: their own remembrance. The witnesses note the what the LORD did in Egypt: his judgments on Pharaoh. The witnesses note the memory the spur: the seen mercy binding to obey (Rom. 12:1). The expositors observe the called all Israel: the whole people summoned. The expositors observe the ye have seen: their own remembrance. The expositors observe the what the LORD did in Egypt: his judgments on Pharaoh. The expositors observe the memory the spur: the seen mercy binding to obey (Rom. 12:1). The believer is taught to mark the called all Israel, the ye have seen, the what the LORD did in Egypt, the memory the spur, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that Moses called all Israel and rehearsed what they had seen in Egypt — the covenant grounded in remembered mercy, the witnessed works of God the spur to obedience (Rom. 12:1).
3:
"The great temptations which thine eyes have seen, the signs, and those great miracles:" The witnesses note the great trials and miracles of Egypt, witnessed by their own eyes. The witnesses note that Gill notes the great trials — the ten plagues which tried the Egyptians whether they would let Israel go, and tried Israel whether they would believe and trust God's power. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the great temptations, signs, and miracles. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the great temptations — the plagues as trials; the thine eyes have seen — witnessed evidence; the signs and great miracles — the proof of God's power; the without excuse — abundant ground for faith (Ps. 78:11).

The witnesses agree the recalling of the "great temptations, signs, and miracles" magnifies the abundant proof God had given of his power and care: the plagues were trials of both Egypt and Israel, and the signs displayed before their eyes left them without excuse for unbelief; the heaping-up of witnessed wonders shows how richly God had attested himself to his people, that their faith and obedience might rest on sure and seen evidence; it teaches that God does not leave himself without witness, but gives his people abundant ground for trust, the remembered miracles a foundation for faith (cf. Ps. 78:11, they forgat his works, and his wonders).

So the witnesses make the verse signs and miracles: The witnesses note the great temptations: the plagues as trials. The witnesses note the thine eyes have seen: witnessed evidence. The witnesses note the signs and great miracles: the proof of God's power. The witnesses note the without excuse: abundant ground for faith (Ps. 78:11). The expositors observe the great temptations: the plagues as trials. The expositors observe the thine eyes have seen: witnessed evidence. The expositors observe the signs and great miracles: the proof of God's power. The expositors observe the without excuse: abundant ground for faith (Ps. 78:11). The believer is taught to mark the great temptations, the thine eyes have seen, the signs and great miracles, the without excuse, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that they had seen the great trials, signs, and miracles of Egypt — God richly attesting his power, leaving them without excuse, the witnessed wonders a foundation for faith (Ps. 78:11).
4:
"Yet the LORD hath not given you an heart to perceive, and eyes to see, and ears to hear, unto this day." The witnesses note that, despite all they had seen, God had not yet given them a heart to truly perceive. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had seen the miracles with their bodily eyes, but had not discerned with the eyes of their understanding the power and goodness of God in them. The witnesses note that they lacked a heart to perceive unto this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not given an heart — the inward eye lacking; the to perceive — beyond the bodily sight; the eyes and ears — the spiritual discernment wanting; the a gift of grace — none knowing without a heart from above (1 Cor. 2:14).

The witnesses agree this verse lays bare the deep truth of man's need of grace even to understand: though Israel had seen God's mighty works, they had "not... an heart to perceive" — wanting the inward eye that discerns God in his deeds, which God alone can give; the bodily sight availed nothing without the spiritual; it teaches that the natural man, for all his outward advantages, cannot truly know God's things without a heart from above, "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God... neither can he know them" (1 Cor. 2:14), the seeing eye and hearing ear a gift of sovereign grace (cf. Prov. 20:12).

So the witnesses make the verse no heart to perceive: The witnesses note the not given an heart: the inward eye lacking. The witnesses note the to perceive: beyond the bodily sight. The witnesses note the eyes and ears: the spiritual discernment wanting. The witnesses note the a gift of grace: none knowing without a heart from above (1 Cor. 2:14). The expositors observe the not given an heart: the inward eye lacking. The expositors observe the to perceive: beyond the bodily sight. The expositors observe the eyes and ears: the spiritual discernment wanting. The expositors observe the a gift of grace: none knowing without a heart from above (1 Cor. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the not given an heart, the to perceive, the eyes and ears, the a gift of grace, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that God had not yet given them a heart to perceive — man's need of grace even to understand, the bodily sight availing nothing without the heart from above, the seeing eye a gift of sovereign grace (1 Cor. 2:14).
5:
"And I have led you forty years in the wilderness: your clothes are not waxen old upon you, and thy shoe is not waxen old upon thy foot." The witnesses note God's sustained care through forty years — their clothes and shoes not wearing out. The witnesses note that Gill notes from their coming out of Egypt to that day, a round number of forty years, the heathen writer Eupolemus confirming the date of Moses' ministry. The witnesses note that their clothes and shoes did not wear out. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the led forty years — through the wilderness; the clothes not old — a standing miracle; the shoe not old — care for the least need; the lacking nothing — god's constant keeping (Deut. 2:7).

The witnesses agree the forty years' preservation was a standing miracle of God's care: through all that long journeying their clothes and shoes "waxen not old," a continual provision that nature could not give; the sustained mercy, reaching to the least of their needs, shows that God who led them also kept them, lacking nothing through the wilderness; it teaches that the Lord's care for his people is constant and complete, watching over every need across the long way, "he knoweth thy walking through this great wilderness... thou hast lacked nothing" (Deut. 2:7), the unworn garment a token of his faithful keeping.

So the witnesses make the verse forty years' care: The witnesses note the led forty years: through the wilderness. The witnesses note the clothes not old: a standing miracle. The witnesses note the shoe not old: care for the least need. The witnesses note the lacking nothing: God's constant keeping (Deut. 2:7). The expositors observe the led forty years: through the wilderness. The expositors observe the clothes not old: a standing miracle. The expositors observe the shoe not old: care for the least need. The expositors observe the lacking nothing: God's constant keeping (Deut. 2:7). The believer is taught to mark the led forty years, the clothes not old, the shoe not old, the lacking nothing, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that their clothes and shoes did not wear out in forty years — a standing miracle of God's sustained care, his keeping reaching to the least need, his people lacking nothing through the wilderness (Deut. 2:7).
6:
"Ye have not eaten bread, neither have ye drunk wine or strong drink: that ye might know that I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note that, fed not on common bread but on manna, they might know the LORD to be their God. The witnesses note that Gill notes they ate not bread of their own making by the labour of their hands, but manna, the food of angels, the bread of heaven. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the end — that ye might know that I am the LORD your God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not eaten bread — no common food; the but manna — the bread of heaven; the daily dependence — cast wholly on God; the that ye might know — the LORD their sustainer (John 6:32).

The witnesses agree the miraculous feeding had a teaching purpose — to make God known: living not on the bread and wine of common labour but on manna, "the bread of heaven," they were taught by their daily dependence "that I am the LORD your God"; the want of ordinary means cast them wholly on God's provision, that they might know him as their sustainer; it teaches that God sometimes withholds the usual supports to make himself known as the giver of all, the daily manna a lesson that man lives by God's word and gift, the heavenly bread pointing to Christ, "the true bread from heaven" (John 6:32).

So the witnesses make the verse that ye might know: The witnesses note the not eaten bread: no common food. The witnesses note the but manna: the bread of heaven. The witnesses note the daily dependence: cast wholly on God. The witnesses note the that ye might know: the LORD their sustainer (John 6:32). The expositors observe the not eaten bread: no common food. The expositors observe the but manna: the bread of heaven. The expositors observe the daily dependence: cast wholly on God. The expositors observe the that ye might know: the LORD their sustainer (John 6:32). The believer is taught to mark the not eaten bread, the but manna, the daily dependence, the that ye might know, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that they ate manna, not common bread, that they might know the LORD their God — the daily dependence teaching him their sustainer, the heavenly bread pointing to Christ the true bread from heaven (John 6:32).
7:
"And when ye came unto this place, Sihon the king of Heshbon, and Og the king of Bashan, came out against us unto battle, and we smote them:" The witnesses note the recent victory over Sihon and Og as a fresh proof of God's goodness. The witnesses note that Gill notes their coming to the borders of Moab, the wilderness before it. The witnesses note that Calvin marks this a third instance of God's goodness, in smiting the first enemies who impeded their passage, the conquest already begun. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came unto this place — the borders of Moab; the Sihon and Og — the two kings out to battle; the we smote them — the conquest begun; the a fresh proof — god's help, hitherto and onward (1 Sam. 7:12).

The witnesses agree the defeat of Sihon and Og was rehearsed as the latest token of God's power for his people: the two kings came out to battle and were smitten, the conquest of the land begun; Calvin reckons it "a third instance" of God's goodness, his help shown afresh in clearing the first foes that barred their way; the recent victory, joined to the older mercies of Egypt and the wilderness, builds up the people's ground of confidence; it teaches that God's past and present deliverances together are the warrant of faith for what lies ahead, the begun conquest a pledge of its completion (cf. 1 Sam. 7:12, hitherto hath the LORD helped us).

So the witnesses make the verse Sihon and Og smitten: The witnesses note the came unto this place: the borders of Moab. The witnesses note the Sihon and Og: the two kings out to battle. The witnesses note the we smote them: the conquest begun. The witnesses note the a fresh proof: God's help, hitherto and onward (1 Sam. 7:12). The expositors observe the came unto this place: the borders of Moab. The expositors observe the Sihon and Og: the two kings out to battle. The expositors observe the we smote them: the conquest begun. The expositors observe the a fresh proof: God's help, hitherto and onward (1 Sam. 7:12). The believer is taught to mark the came unto this place, the Sihon and Og, the we smote them, the a fresh proof, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that Sihon and Og were smitten — the latest token of God's goodness, the conquest begun, his present and past help together the warrant of faith for what lay ahead (1 Sam. 7:12).
8:
"And we took their land, and gave it for an inheritance unto the Reubenites, and to the Gadites, and to the half tribe of Manasseh." The witnesses note the conquered land given as an inheritance to the two tribes and a half. The witnesses note that Gill notes the lands of Jazer, Gilead, and Bashan, fine countries for pasturage, belonging to the two kings. The witnesses note that they were given to Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took their land — the conquered countries; the for an inheritance — the first portion possessed; the Reuben, Gad, half Manasseh — settled in their own; the the firstfruits — a pledge of the whole (2 Cor. 1:22).

The witnesses agree the giving of the conquered land was the first earnest of the promised inheritance: the rich countries of the two kings were "given for an inheritance" to Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh, the first portion of the people settled in their own; the begun fulfilment of the promise — land actually possessed — was a pledge that God would give the rest; it teaches that the firstfruits of God's promises are a sure token of their full performance, the first inheritance a guarantee of the whole, "who hath given us the earnest of the Spirit" (2 Cor. 1:22), the part assuring the all.

So the witnesses make the verse the land given: The witnesses note the took their land: the conquered countries. The witnesses note the for an inheritance: the first portion possessed. The witnesses note the Reuben, Gad, half Manasseh: settled in their own. The witnesses note the the firstfruits: a pledge of the whole (2 Cor. 1:22). The expositors observe the took their land: the conquered countries. The expositors observe the for an inheritance: the first portion possessed. The expositors observe the Reuben, Gad, half Manasseh: settled in their own. The expositors observe the the firstfruits: a pledge of the whole (2 Cor. 1:22). The believer is taught to mark the took their land, the for an inheritance, the Reuben, Gad, half Manasseh, the the firstfruits, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that the conquered land was given to the two tribes and a half — the first earnest of the inheritance, the begun fulfilment a pledge that God would give the rest, the part assuring the whole (2 Cor. 1:22).
9:
"Keep therefore the words of this covenant, and do them, that ye may prosper in all that ye do." The witnesses note the charge drawn from all God's mercies — keep and do the covenant, that they may prosper. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were laid under great obligations by God's goodness — the victory over the two kings and all the favours in the wilderness. The witnesses note that the keeping of the covenant was the way to prosper. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the covenant — the grateful response; the and do them — obedience, not hearing only; the that ye may prosper — the way of welfare; the the path of blessing — obedience the prosperous way (Josh. 1:8).

The witnesses agree the "therefore" binds obedience to the recital of mercies and joins it to prosperity: because God had so dealt with them, they were to "keep... and do" the covenant, and to this is annexed the promise "that ye may prosper"; the obedience is both the grateful response to grace and the appointed way of welfare; it teaches that the keeping of God's covenant is no barren duty but the path of blessing, gratitude and prosperity meeting in obedience, "this book of the law... thou shalt meditate therein... for then thou shalt make thy way prosperous" (Josh. 1:8).

So the witnesses make the verse keep and prosper: The witnesses note the keep the covenant: the grateful response. The witnesses note the and do them: obedience, not hearing only. The witnesses note the that ye may prosper: the way of welfare. The witnesses note the the path of blessing: obedience the prosperous way (Josh. 1:8). The expositors observe the keep the covenant: the grateful response. The expositors observe the and do them: obedience, not hearing only. The expositors observe the that ye may prosper: the way of welfare. The expositors observe the the path of blessing: obedience the prosperous way (Josh. 1:8). The believer is taught to mark the keep the covenant, the and do them, the that ye may prosper, the the path of blessing, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that they were to keep and do the covenant, that they might prosper — obedience the grateful response to grace and the appointed way of welfare, the keeping of God's covenant the path of blessing (Josh. 1:8).
10:
"Ye stand this day all of you before the LORD your God; your captains of your tribes, your elders, and your officers, with all the men of Israel," The witnesses note all Israel, of every rank, standing before the LORD to enter the covenant. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were gathered at the door of the tabernacle; Aben Ezra, round about the ark, the symbol of the divine Presence. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set all of them standing before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye stand this day — before the LORD; the captains and elders — the highest ranks; the all the men of Israel — none excepted; the all alike his — one before God in the covenant (Gal. 3:28).

The witnesses agree the whole nation, from the highest to the lowest, stood together before God to enter the covenant: captains, elders, officers, "with all the men of Israel," the entire people gathered before the divine Presence; the standing of all ranks together shows that the covenant embraced every member of the nation, none excepted by station; it teaches that all God's people, great and small, stand on level ground before him in the bond of his covenant, "ye are all one in Christ Jesus" (Gal. 3:28), the whole congregation alike his.

So the witnesses make the verse all stand before God: The witnesses note the ye stand this day: before the LORD. The witnesses note the captains and elders: the highest ranks. The witnesses note the all the men of Israel: none excepted. The witnesses note the all alike his: one before God in the covenant (Gal. 3:28). The expositors observe the ye stand this day: before the LORD. The expositors observe the captains and elders: the highest ranks. The expositors observe the all the men of Israel: none excepted. The expositors observe the all alike his: one before God in the covenant (Gal. 3:28). The believer is taught to mark the ye stand this day, the captains and elders, the all the men of Israel, the all alike his, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that all Israel, of every rank, stood before the LORD to enter the covenant — the whole nation embraced, none excepted by station, all alike his in the bond of his covenant (Gal. 3:28).
11:
"Your little ones, your wives, and thy stranger that is in thy camp, from the hewer of thy wood unto the drawer of thy water:" The witnesses note the covenant reaching to the lowest — little ones, wives, strangers, even the wood-hewer and water-drawer. The witnesses note that Gill notes the little ones and wives, scarce ever mentioned in any special law or solemn transaction. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the covenant embraced even the stranger and the meanest servants. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the little ones and wives — the least regarded; the thy stranger — the foreigner in the camp; the hewer of wood, drawer of water — the meanest servants; the grace despises none — the promise to you and your children (Acts 2:39).

The witnesses agree the covenant's breadth is here shown most strikingly, reaching to the very least: not the captains and elders only, but "little ones, wives, the stranger... the hewer of wood, the drawer of water" — the humblest and least regarded — were embraced; Gill marks that women and children, "scarce ever mentioned" in solemn transactions, are here included; the reaching of the covenant to the lowest shows that God's grace despises none, the meanest as truly within the bond as the greatest; it teaches that in his covenant "there is neither bond nor free," the poor and the stranger gathered in with the rest (cf. Acts 2:39, the promise is unto you, and to your children).

So the witnesses make the verse down to the lowest: The witnesses note the little ones and wives: the least regarded. The witnesses note the thy stranger: the foreigner in the camp. The witnesses note the hewer of wood, drawer of water: the meanest servants. The witnesses note the grace despises none: the promise to you and your children (Acts 2:39). The expositors observe the little ones and wives: the least regarded. The expositors observe the thy stranger: the foreigner in the camp. The expositors observe the hewer of wood, drawer of water: the meanest servants. The expositors observe the grace despises none: the promise to you and your children (Acts 2:39). The believer is taught to mark the little ones and wives, the thy stranger, the hewer of wood, drawer of water, the grace despises none, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that the covenant reached to the little ones, wives, strangers, and meanest servants — God's grace despising none, the lowest as truly within the bond as the greatest, the promise unto you and your children (Acts 2:39).
12:
"That thou shouldest enter into covenant with the LORD thy God, and into his oath, which the LORD thy God maketh with thee this day:" The witnesses note the purpose of their standing — to enter into covenant and oath with the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to appear and stand to solemnly avouch him their God and hear him declare them his people. The witnesses note that the covenant and oath were made with them that day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the enter into covenant — the sworn engagement; the and into his oath — the deep solemnity; the made this day — the bond sealed; the confirmed by oath — two immutable things (Heb. 6:18).

The witnesses agree the great purpose of the assembly was a solemn, sworn engagement between God and his people: they stood "to enter into covenant... and into his oath," binding themselves to him and receiving his binding promise to them; the joining of an "oath" to the covenant marks the deep solemnity and surety of the transaction, God confirming his word with an oath and the people pledging their allegiance; it teaches that the bond between God and his own is no light thing but a sworn covenant, sealed on both sides, the surety of God's promise resting on his oath, "that by two immutable things... we might have a strong consolation" (Heb. 6:18).

So the witnesses make the verse enter the covenant: The witnesses note the enter into covenant: the sworn engagement. The witnesses note the and into his oath: the deep solemnity. The witnesses note the made this day: the bond sealed. The witnesses note the confirmed by oath: two immutable things (Heb. 6:18). The expositors observe the enter into covenant: the sworn engagement. The expositors observe the and into his oath: the deep solemnity. The expositors observe the made this day: the bond sealed. The expositors observe the confirmed by oath: two immutable things (Heb. 6:18). The believer is taught to mark the enter into covenant, the and into his oath, the made this day, the confirmed by oath, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that they stood to enter into covenant and oath with the LORD — a solemn, sworn engagement on both sides, the surety of God's promise confirmed by his oath (Heb. 6:18).
13:
"That he may establish thee to day for a people unto himself, and that he may be unto thee a God, as he hath said unto thee, and as he hath sworn unto thy fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob." The witnesses note the heart of the covenant — that God establish them his people and be their God, as sworn to the fathers. The witnesses note that Gill notes this contains the sum and substance of the covenant. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the establishing of them as a people unto himself, as sworn to the fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the establish thee a people — his own possession; the he may be thy God — the mutual belonging; the as sworn to the fathers — grounded in the oath; the the heart of the covenant — i will be their God (Jer. 31:33).

The witnesses agree this verse states "the sum and substance of the covenant" — the mutual belonging of God and his people: that he might "establish thee... for a people unto himself, and... be unto thee a God"; the reciprocal promise — they his people, he their God — is the very heart of all covenant grace, grounded in the oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; it is the refrain of Scripture's covenant, "I will be their God, and they shall be my people" (Jer. 31:33), fulfilled at last in the church, where God dwells with his own and is their God forever.

So the witnesses make the verse his people, their God: The witnesses note the establish thee a people: his own possession. The witnesses note the he may be thy God: the mutual belonging. The witnesses note the as sworn to the fathers: grounded in the oath. The witnesses note the the heart of the covenant: I will be their God (Jer. 31:33). The expositors observe the establish thee a people: his own possession. The expositors observe the he may be thy God: the mutual belonging. The expositors observe the as sworn to the fathers: grounded in the oath. The expositors observe the the heart of the covenant: I will be their God (Jer. 31:33). The believer is taught to mark the establish thee a people, the he may be thy God, the as sworn to the fathers, the the heart of the covenant, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that God would establish them his people and be their God, as sworn to the fathers — the sum of the covenant, the mutual belonging fulfilled at last in the church, I will be their God and they my people (Jer. 31:33).
14:
"Neither with you only do I make this covenant and this oath;" The witnesses note that the covenant was not with the present generation only. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses was ordered to make this covenant in the name of the Lord, delivering his promises and requirements. The witnesses note that it was not with them only. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this covenant and oath — the sworn bond; the neither with you only — not the present alone; the a wider reach — beyond the assembly; the down the generations — to your children afar off (Acts 2:39).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the covenant's reach beyond the present assembly: "neither with you only" — the bond was not confined to those then standing, but extended further; the breadth shown prepares for the next verse, that generations yet unborn were included; it teaches that God's covenant is not bound to one age but reaches down the generations, embracing the children not yet present, the promise made "to you, and to your children, and to all that are afar off" (Acts 2:39), the covenant of grace overleaping time to gather all God's own.

So the witnesses make the verse not with you only: The witnesses note the this covenant and oath: the sworn bond. The witnesses note the neither with you only: not the present alone. The witnesses note the a wider reach: beyond the assembly. The witnesses note the down the generations: to your children afar off (Acts 2:39). The expositors observe the this covenant and oath: the sworn bond. The expositors observe the neither with you only: not the present alone. The expositors observe the a wider reach: beyond the assembly. The expositors observe the down the generations: to your children afar off (Acts 2:39). The believer is taught to mark the this covenant and oath, the neither with you only, the a wider reach, the down the generations, and to keep the covenant of his God. The verse thus records that the covenant was not with the present generation only — God's bond reaching beyond the assembly, down the generations, the promise overleaping time to gather all his own (Acts 2:39).
15:
"But with him that standeth here with us this day before the LORD our God, and also with him that is not here with us this day:" The witnesses note the covenant made with those present and with those not yet born. The witnesses note that Gill notes those present are specified by dignity, age, sex, and station; the others are those not yet in being. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the covenant with him that is not here this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the him that standeth here — the present generation; the also him not here — those not yet born; the the seed embraced — future generations bound; the perpetual covenant — to children's children (Ps. 103:17).

The witnesses agree the covenant reached the unborn generations as truly as the living: it was made "with him that standeth here" and "with him that is not here" — those not yet in being, the children of ages to come; the binding of future generations shows that God's covenant is a thing of perpetual obligation and perpetual grace, the seed embraced with the fathers; it teaches that the promises and the duties of the covenant pass on to the children, who are born into its bond, "the mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting upon them that fear him... and his righteousness unto children's children" (Ps. 103:17).

So the witnesses make the verse with the unborn too: The witnesses note the him that standeth here: the present generation. The witnesses note the also him not here: those not yet born. The witnesses note the the seed embraced: future generations bound. The witnesses note the perpetual covenant: to children's children (Ps. 103:17). 16:
"(For ye know how we have dwelt in the land of Egypt; and how we came through the nations which ye passed by;" The witnesses note the reminder of their long dwelling in Egypt and their passage through idolatrous nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes how long they and their fathers had dwelt in Egypt, upwards of two hundred years, a land much given to idolatry. The witnesses note that they had passed through other nations also. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the dwelt in Egypt — among idolaters long; the through the nations — idolatrous lands; the exposed to the contagion — of false worship; the on their guard — come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17).

The witnesses agree the reminder of Egypt and the nations sets the ground of the warning to follow: having dwelt long among idolaters and passed through idolatrous lands, Israel had been much exposed to the contagion of false worship; the recalling of this danger prepares for the charge against any heart that might turn aside; it teaches that the people of God, having lived among the corruptions of the world, are in special peril of being infected by them, and must be on their guard against the idolatry they have seen, "come out from among them, and be ye separate" (2 Cor. 6:17).

So the witnesses make the verse exposed to idolatry: The witnesses note the dwelt in Egypt: among idolaters long. The witnesses note the through the nations: idolatrous lands. The witnesses note the exposed to the contagion: of false worship. The witnesses note the on their guard: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The expositors observe the dwelt in Egypt: among idolaters long. The expositors observe the through the nations: idolatrous lands. The expositors observe the exposed to the contagion: of false worship. The expositors observe the on their guard: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The verse thus records that they had dwelt in Egypt and passed through idolatrous nations — exposed to the contagion of false worship, in special peril of its infection, called to be on guard and separate (2 Cor. 6:17).
17:
"And ye have seen their abominations, and their idols, wood and stone, silver and gold, which were among them:)" The witnesses note that they had seen the idols of the nations — abominations of wood, stone, silver, and gold. The witnesses note that Gill notes the idols are called "abominations," abominable to God and to be so to men. The witnesses note that they were of wood and stone, silver and gold. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the their abominations — the idols of the nations; the wood and stone — mere lifeless matter; the silver and gold — dead things adored; the the folly of idolatry — the work of men's hands (Ps. 115:4).

The witnesses agree the naming of the idols as "abominations" of mere "wood and stone, silver and gold" marks their vanity and the folly of worshipping them: the people had seen these lifeless things adored among the nations, and were to abhor them as God does; the calling of idols "abominations" throughout Scripture shows how hateful false worship is to God and how worthless its objects — dead matter set in the place of the living God; it teaches the utter folly of idolatry, that men should bow to "the work of men's hands... they have mouths, but they speak not" (Ps. 115:4), the people to despise what they had seen.

So the witnesses make the verse idols of wood and stone: The witnesses note the their abominations: the idols of the nations. The witnesses note the wood and stone: mere lifeless matter. The witnesses note the silver and gold: dead things adored. The witnesses note the the folly of idolatry: the work of men's hands (Ps. 115:4). The verse thus records that they had seen the idols, abominations of wood and stone — the vanity and folly of idolatry, lifeless matter set in the place of the living God, the work of men's hands (Ps. 115:4).
18:
"Lest there should be among you man, or woman, or family, or tribe, whose heart turneth away this day from the LORD our God... a root that beareth gall and wormwood;" The witnesses note the warning against any whose heart secretly turns from God — a root bearing gall and wormwood. The witnesses note that Gill notes these words show the design of the solemn covenant — to prevent their falling into idolatry and preserve them from it. The witnesses note that such a one is a root bearing bitter herbs. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the man, woman, family, tribe — any quarter at risk; the whose heart turneth away — the secret apostasy; the gall and wormwood — a bitter, spreading evil; the vigilance over the heart — lest a root of bitterness defile many (Heb. 12:15).

The witnesses agree the great purpose of the covenant-renewal was to guard against secret apostasy in any quarter — "man, or woman, or family, or tribe": a heart turned from God is "a root that beareth gall and wormwood," a hidden source of bitter and spreading evil; the figure marks how one apostate heart can poison many, the secret turning bearing deadly fruit; it teaches the danger of hidden declension and the duty of vigilance over the heart, lest "any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled" (Heb. 12:15), the covenant guarding against the first inward turning.

So the witnesses make the verse a root of bitterness: The witnesses note the man, woman, family, tribe: any quarter at risk. The witnesses note the whose heart turneth away: the secret apostasy. The witnesses note the gall and wormwood: a bitter, spreading evil. The witnesses note the vigilance over the heart: lest a root of bitterness defile many (Heb. 12:15). 19:
"And it come to pass, when he heareth the words of this curse, that he bless himself in his heart, saying, I shall have peace, though I walk in the imagination of mine heart, to add drunkenness to thirst:" The witnesses note the presumptuous man who, hearing the curse, flatters himself he shall have peace though he walks in his own way. The witnesses note that Gill notes the man before compared to a root of bitter herbs, hearing the curses pronounced against such as himself. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him blessing himself in his heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the heareth the curse — yet unmoved by it; the bless himself in his heart — the self-deception; the I shall have peace — a false security in sin; the peace where none is — crying peace, peace (Jer. 6:14).

The witnesses agree the verse exposes the deadly self-deception of the presumptuous sinner: hearing the very curse, he yet "blesses himself," persuading his heart "I shall have peace, though I walk in the imagination of mine heart"; the false peace of one who hardens himself against warning, promising himself safety in sin, is the most dangerous of states; it teaches that men may flatter themselves into ruin, crying "peace, peace; when there is no peace" (Jer. 6:14), the heart deceiving itself against the plain word of God, adding sin to sin in a false security.

So the witnesses make the verse the false peace: The witnesses note the heareth the curse: yet unmoved by it. The witnesses note the bless himself in his heart: the self-deception. 20:
"The LORD will not spare him, but then the anger of the LORD and his jealousy shall smoke against that man, and all the curses... shall lie upon him, and the LORD shall blot out his name from under heaven." The witnesses note the doom of the presumptuous — God will not spare, but his anger shall smoke against him and blot out his name. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord will have no mercy on him, being a hardhearted, impenitent, obstinate sinner, guilty of the most provoking sin. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets the anger that "shall smoke," comparing the Psalms. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the will not spare him — no mercy for the hardened; the his anger shall smoke — wrath against the presumptuous; the the curses lie on him — the full judgment fall; the his name blotted out — the hardened suddenly destroyed (Prov. 29:1).

The witnesses agree the false peace ends in a terrible reckoning: against the self-flattering sinner "the LORD will not spare," but his "anger... and jealousy shall smoke," all the curses fall on him, and his very name be blotted out; the heaping-up of wrath shows that presumption does not escape but invites the heaviest judgment, the hardened heart finding no mercy; it teaches that those who promise themselves peace in sin shall meet the full curse instead, "he that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed" (Prov. 29:1), the name of the presumptuous blotted out.

So the witnesses make the verse God will not spare: The witnesses note the will not spare him: no mercy for the hardened. The witnesses note the his anger shall smoke: wrath against the presumptuous. 21:
"And the LORD shall separate him unto evil out of all the tribes of Israel, according to all the curses of the covenant that are written in this book of the law:" The witnesses note the apostate singled out and devoted to judgment from among all the tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord devotes and consigns him to the evil of punishment, making him a visible mark of his displeasure and vengeance. The witnesses note that this was according to all the curses written in the book. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the separate him — singled out for judgment; the out of all the tribes — from among God's people; the unto evil — a mark of displeasure; the the false parted — the tares from the wheat (Matt. 13:30).

The witnesses agree the apostate is solemnly marked out for judgment from among the people of God: the LORD "shall separate him unto evil out of all the tribes," singling him from the rest as a visible monument of his displeasure, under all the covenant's curses; the setting-apart of the offender shows that membership among God's people is no shield to the apostate, who is rather distinguished for the heavier doom; it teaches that the false within the visible church are at last separated to judgment, the wheat and tares parted, the offender consigned to "the curses of the covenant" he despised (cf. Matt. 13:30).

So the witnesses make the verse separated unto evil: The witnesses note the separate him: singled out for judgment. The witnesses note the out of all the tribes: from among God's people. 22:
"So that the generation to come of your children that shall rise up after you, and the stranger that shall come from a far land, shall say, when they see the plagues of that land..." The witnesses note that future generations and far nations would marvel at the desolation of the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes not the next generation but future ages, even after the destruction of Judea by the Romans. The witnesses note that Calvin marks that God enforces the certainty of the punishment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the generation to come — future ages; the the stranger from afar — distant lands; the see the plagues — the awful desolation; the a public testimony — an astonishment among nations (Deut. 28:37).

The witnesses agree the desolation foretold would be so awful as to become a wonder to later ages and distant lands: "the generation to come" and "the stranger from a far land" would see "the plagues of that land" and be astonished; the appeal to future witnesses presses the certainty and the publicity of the judgment, that God's wrath against covenant-breaking would be displayed before the world; it teaches that the chastisement of God's people is a public testimony to his holiness, "an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword, among all nations" (Deut. 28:37), the desolate land a lasting witness.

23:
"And that the whole land thereof is brimstone, and salt, and burning... like the overthrow of Sodom, and Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim, which the LORD overthrew in his anger:" The witnesses note the land made a barren waste like Sodom, overthrown in God's anger. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land brimstone and salt, not sown nor bearing. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the likeness to the overthrow of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brimstone and salt — barren and burning; the not sown nor bearing — a dreadful waste; the like Sodom's overthrow — the cities God destroyed; the a monument of wrath — set forth for an example (Jude 7).

The witnesses agree the desolation is likened to the most dreadful judgment in their history — the overthrow of Sodom: the land would become "brimstone, and salt, and burning," barren and unsown, "like the overthrow of Sodom"; the comparison marks the severity of the judgment on covenant-breaking, equalling the doom of the cities God destroyed in his anger; it teaches that God's own people, if they forsake him, may be made a like monument of his wrath, the fruitful land turned to a Sodom, "set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal fire" (Jude 7), a warning to all.

So the witnesses make the verse like Sodom: The witnesses note the brimstone and salt: barren and burning. The witnesses note the not sown nor bearing: a dreadful waste. The witnesses note the like Sodom's overthrow: the cities God destroyed. The witnesses note the a monument of wrath: set forth for an example (Jude 7). The verse thus records that the land would be made a barren waste like Sodom — the severity of judgment on covenant-breaking, the fruitful land turned to a monument of wrath, set forth for an example (Jude 7).
24:
"Even all nations shall say, Wherefore hath the LORD done thus unto this land? what meaneth the heat of this great anger?" The witnesses note the nations asking why the LORD had so terribly destroyed the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the destruction would be so great and awful, so remarkable, that its fame would be heard among all nations, who would ask the question. The witnesses note that they would ask the meaning of his great anger. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all nations shall say — the heathen inquiring; the wherefore done thus — seeking the cause; the the heat of his anger — no chance but God's act; the judgment preaches — the LORD known by his judgments (Ps. 9:16).

The witnesses agree the very heathen would be moved to ask the cause of so signal a judgment: the destruction so "great and awful" that "all nations" inquire, "wherefore hath the LORD done thus?"; the question itself bears witness that such desolation is no chance but the act of an angry God, demanding an explanation; it teaches that God's judgments on his people preach to the world, raising the question of his holiness and their sin, that men might learn to fear, "the LORD is known by the judgment which he executeth" (Ps. 9:16), the very heathen made to seek the reason.

So the witnesses make the verse wherefore done thus: The witnesses note the all nations shall say: the heathen inquiring. The witnesses note the wherefore done thus: seeking the cause. The verse thus records that the nations would ask why the LORD so destroyed the land — God's judgment preaching to the world, the very heathen seeking the cause, the LORD known by the judgment he executes (Ps. 9:16).
25:
"Then men shall say, Because they have forsaken the covenant of the LORD God of their fathers, which he made with them when he brought them forth out of the land of Egypt:" The witnesses note the answer to the nations' question — they had forsaken the covenant of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the answer returned to the questions. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the reason — because they forsook the covenant the LORD made when he brought them out of Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the men shall say — the answer given; the forsaken the covenant — their unfaithfulness; the the LORD of their fathers — who redeemed them from Egypt; the basest ingratitude — they forgat God their saviour (Ps. 106:21).

The witnesses agree the answer fixes the cause of the judgment squarely on the people's covenant-breaking: "because they have forsaken the covenant" which God made in redeeming them from Egypt; the desolation was the just recompense of their unfaithfulness to so gracious a bond; it teaches that the root of all the judgment was their forsaking of the God who had redeemed them, the basest ingratitude met with righteous wrath, "they... forgat God their saviour, which had done great things in Egypt" (Ps. 106:21), the broken covenant the cause of the ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse they forsook the covenant: The witnesses note the men shall say: the answer given. The witnesses note the forsaken the covenant: their unfaithfulness. The witnesses note the the LORD of their fathers: who redeemed them from Egypt. The witnesses note the basest ingratitude: they forgat God their saviour (Ps. 106:21). The expositors observe the men shall say: the answer given. The expositors observe the forsaken the covenant: their unfaithfulness. The expositors observe the the LORD of their fathers: who redeemed them from Egypt. The expositors observe the basest ingratitude: they forgat God their saviour (Ps. 106:21). The verse thus records that the cause was their forsaking the covenant the LORD made in redeeming them — the judgment the just recompense of unfaithfulness, the basest ingratitude to the God who saved them (Ps. 106:21).
26:
"For they went and served other gods, and worshipped them, gods whom they knew not, and whom he had not given unto them:" The witnesses note the specific sin — they went and served other gods, unknown and unallotted to them. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was done by all Israel in Solomon's time, the ten tribes under Jeroboam, and Judah under Ahaz and Manasseh, worshipping the host of heaven. The witnesses note that they served gods whom they knew not. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went and served — the act of idolatry; the other gods — forsaking the true; the whom they knew not — unknown vanities; the the fountain forsaken — for broken cisterns (Jer. 2:13).

The witnesses agree the covenant-breaking is named plainly as idolatry: "they went and served other gods... whom they knew not, and whom he had not given unto them"; the folly is marked — forsaking the God who had revealed himself and done such things, for gods utterly unknown and never appointed them; Gill traces the sin through Israel's history, from Solomon to Manasseh; it teaches that idolatry is at root the forsaking of the known and giving God for unknown vanities, "my people have... forsaken me the fountain of living waters, and hewed them out... broken cisterns" (Jer. 2:13), the cause of all the ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse served other gods: The witnesses note the went and served: the act of idolatry. The witnesses note the other gods: forsaking the true. The witnesses note the whom they knew not: unknown vanities. The witnesses note the the fountain forsaken: for broken cisterns (Jer. 2:13). 27:
"And the anger of the LORD was kindled against this land, to bring upon it all the curses that are written in this book:" The witnesses note God's anger kindled against the land, to bring on it all the written curses. The witnesses note that Gill notes the anger for their idolatry and base ingratitude. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the anger kindled to bring all the curses of the book upon the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the anger kindled — against the land; the all the curses — of the written book; the fully performed — no empty threat; the the warnings sure — the word shall judge (John 12:48).

The witnesses agree the verse declares the certain execution of the threatened curses: God's anger "was kindled" to bring upon the land "all the curses that are written in this book"; the threatenings were no empty words but would be fully performed, every curse of the law made good upon the impenitent; it teaches that God's warnings are as sure as his promises, and that the despised curses of his word will surely overtake those who break his covenant, "the word that I have spoken... shall judge him" (John 12:48), the whole book of curses fulfilled.

So the witnesses make the verse the curses brought: The witnesses note the anger kindled: against the land. The witnesses note the all the curses: of the written book. The witnesses note the fully performed: no empty threat. The witnesses note the the warnings sure: the word shall judge (John 12:48). The expositors observe the anger kindled: against the land. 28:
"And the LORD rooted them out of their land in anger, and in wrath, and in great indignation, and cast them into another land, as it is this day." The witnesses note the rooting-out of the people from their land in God's wrath, into captivity. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was true both at the Babylonish captivity and at their present dispersion by the Romans, by whom they have been rooted out these 1700 years. The witnesses note that they were cast into another land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rooted them out — torn from the land; the in anger and wrath — the severity of judgment; the cast into another land — into captivity and dispersion; the a lasting witness — led captive into all nations (Luke 21:24).

The witnesses agree the verse foretells the uprooting and exile of the people — fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity and yet more in the long Roman dispersion: God "rooted them out... in anger" and "cast them into another land, as it is this day"; the heaping of "anger, wrath, and great indignation" marks the severity of the judgment, the people torn from their inheritance for their sin; it teaches that persistent covenant-breaking brings not chastening only but uprooting, the standing dispersion of Israel a visible and lasting witness to the truth of God's threatening (cf. Luke 21:24, led away captive into all nations).

So the witnesses make the verse rooted out: The witnesses note the rooted them out: torn from the land. The witnesses note the in anger and wrath: the severity of judgment. The witnesses note the cast into another land: into captivity and dispersion. The witnesses note the a lasting witness: led captive into all nations (Luke 21:24). The expositors observe the rooted them out: torn from the land. The expositors observe the in anger and wrath: the severity of judgment. The expositors observe the cast into another land: into captivity and dispersion. The expositors observe the a lasting witness: led captive into all nations (Luke 21:24). The verse thus records that the LORD rooted them out in wrath and cast them into another land — covenant-breaking bringing uprooting and exile, the long dispersion a lasting witness to the truth of his threatening (Luke 21:24).
29:
"The secret things belong unto the LORD our God: but those things which are revealed belong unto us and to our children for ever, that we may do all the words of this law." The witnesses note the great distinction — secret things are God's, but revealed things are ours, that we may obey. The witnesses note that Gill notes the secret things respect God's providential dealings with Israel, especially their final rejection, like the apostle's exclamation at the depth of God's judgments. The witnesses note that the revealed things belong to us and our children, that we may do the law. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the secret things — god's hidden counsels; the belong to the LORD — not for us to pry; the the revealed things — given to us and our children; the that we may obey — doing all the words of the law (Rom. 11:33).

The witnesses agree this great closing verse draws the boundary between God's hidden counsels and his revealed will: "the secret things belong unto the LORD" — his unsearchable purposes, like the deep judgments on Israel, are not for us to pry into; "but those things which are revealed belong unto us and to our children" — given that we may obey; the verse teaches humble submission to what God has hidden and diligent obedience to what he has made known, that we busy ourselves not with curious speculation but with doing his word, "the things revealed" enough for faith and duty (cf. Rom. 11:33, how unsearchable are his judgments), the practical end of all being "that we may do all the words of this law."

So the witnesses make the verse secret and revealed: The witnesses note the the secret things: God's hidden counsels. The witnesses note the belong to the LORD: not for us to pry. The witnesses note the the revealed things: given to us and our children. The witnesses note the that we may obey: doing all the words of the law (Rom. 11:33). The expositors observe the the secret things: God's hidden counsels. The expositors observe the belong to the LORD: not for us to pry. The expositors observe the the revealed things: given to us and our children. The expositors observe the that we may obey: doing all the words of the law (Rom. 11:33). The believer is taught to mark the the secret things, the belong to the LORD, the the revealed things, the that we may obey, and to keep his heart from turning from God. The verse thus records that the secret things are God's but the revealed are ours, that we may obey — humble submission to what he hides and diligent obedience to what he reveals, the things revealed enough for faith and duty (Rom. 11:33).
